138.title: Dishonour of cheque for insufficiency, etc., of funds in the account
138.content: Where any cheque drawn by a person on an account maintained by him with a banker for payment of any amount of money to another person from out of that account for the discharge, in whole or in part, of any debt or other liability, is returned by the bank unpaid...
138.punishment: Imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine which may extend to twice the amount of the cheque, or with both.
conditions: Cheque must be presented within its validity period (3 months)
Demand notice must be sent within 30 days of dishonour
Drawer must fail to pay within 15 days of notice receipt

139.title: Presumption in favour of holder
139.content: It shall be presumed, unless the contrary is proved, that the holder of a cheque received the cheque of the nature referred to in section 138 for the discharge, in whole or in part, of any debt or other liability.
139.interpretation: Mandatory presumption that shifts the burden of proof to the accused to prove 'no debt' by a 'preponderance of probability'.

141.title: Offences by companies
141.content: If the person committing an offence under section 138 is a company, every person who, at the time the offence was committed, was in charge of, and was responsible to the company for the conduct of the business of the company, as well as the company, shall be deemed to be guilty of the offence...
141.requirement: Company must be impleaded as an accused. Specific role attribution for directors is mandatory.

142.title: Cognizance of offences
142.content: No court shall take cognizance of any offence punishable under section 138 except upon a complaint, in writing, made by the payee or, as the case may be, the holder in due course of the cheque...
142.limitation: Complaint must be filed within one month of the date on which the cause of action arises.

143A.title: Power to direct interim compensation
143A.content: The Court trying an offence under section 138 may order the drawer of the cheque to pay interim compensation to the complainant...
143A.limit: Up to 20% of the cheque amount.

cheque_bounce.case: K. Bhaskaran v. Sankaran Vaidhyan Balan
cheque_bounce.principle: Defined the five components of the offence and territorial jurisdiction parameters.

cheque_bounce.case: Laxmi Dyechem v. State of Gujarat
cheque_bounce.principle: Expanded the scope of S.138 to include 'signature mismatch' as a reason for dishonour.
Payee can present the cheque multiple times and issue notice on any dishonour.

cheque_bounce.case: NEPC Micon Ltd. v. Magma Leasing Ltd.
cheque_bounce.principle: Dishonour due to 'account closed' also falls under Section 138.

cheque_bounce.case: Modi Cements Ltd. v. Kuchil Kumar Nandi
cheque_bounce.principle: Stop payment instruction doesn't escape S.138 if debt exists.
Reiterated the mandatory presumption of debt under Section 139.
Dishonour due to account closed attracts Section 138 liability.
Stop payment instructions do not escape criminal liability if debt exists.
Signature mismatch is a valid ground for dishonour under S.138.
Material alteration of date/amount voids the cheque.
Electronic bank memos are valid evidence of dishonour.

presumption_s139.principle: Presumption mandated by Section 139 includes the existence of a legally enforceable debt.
Accused can rebut presumption by questioning financial capacity.
Burden of proof shifts to the accused once signature is admitted.
Preponderance of probability is enough to rebut the statutory presumption.
Security cheque given for a crystallised debt attracts presumption.
Complainant's financial capacity can be questioned.
Rebuttal must be through cogent evidence, not mere denial.

vicarious_liability_issue.principle: Impleading the Company is mandatory for prosecuting its directors.

vicarious_liability_issue.case: SMS Pharmaceuticals Ltd v. Neeta Bhalla
vicarious_liability_issue.principle: Specific averments regarding the role of directors are mandatory.
Managing directors are inherently responsible; others need specific role attribution.
Non-executive directors are not liable unless specific role is proven.
Resigned directors are not liable for cheques issued after resignation.
Impleading the company is a mandatory condition.

security_cheque.principle: Security cheques issued for crystallised debt are covered under S.138.

security_cheque.case: Sripati Singh v. State of Jharkhand
security_cheque.principle: A cheque given as security can be treated as regular if loan is not repaid.
Advance payment security does not attract S.138 if order is cancelled.
Cheque issued as security for a loan is covered if debt is due.
Undated security cheques are valid once filled and presented.
Rebuttal requires proof of no existing debt.

legal_notice_compliance.case: C.C. Alavi Haji v. Palapetty Muhammed
legal_notice_compliance.principle: Notice is deemed served if sent to correct address.

legal_notice_compliance.case: D. Vinod Shivappa v. Nanda Belliappa
legal_notice_compliance.principle: Service of notice is a matter of evidence at trial.
Correct address on notice is sufficient to prove service.
Demand notice must be issued within 30 days.
Defective notice with incorrect amount may invalidate the complaint.

limitation_issue.case: Saketh India Ltd v. India Securities Ltd
limitation_issue.principle: Exclusion of the day cause of action arises for limitation.
Limitation of 1 month starts from 15th day of notice delivery.
Condonation of delay allowed only on showing 'sufficient cause'.
Calculation excludes the day cause of action arose.
